## [03:02 - 06:06]

active people and we just build a graph on top of these people and we have possibility to link to talk about data in perspective of people. So we are not tracking devices, we're not tracking cookies, we're tracking people. So this means for you that you can analyze how people use the product, how people use your product or website or service across different channels, whether they are logged in or not. So the typical use cases are how many users will view my posts or comment on my Facebook posts and then go on to view the website and install the app and then make a purchase in the store. So this is the kind of insight that you can derive from Facebook analytics now. The second aspect aside from omni-channel it's a tool that helps you analyze demographics. So we provide very very rich demographics data including as you can see here age and gender which are like accurate based on Facebook profiles, household incomes, countries, languages, education level, even relationship status and so on and so forth. Obviously all this data is aggregated so we aggregate and analyze demographic data about your audience and this is pretty unique. So when you track an event with Facebook analytics you automatically get the demographics data and location and more by out of the box and then you can use this data and segmentation through the product which we'll get to. The second thing is as I said it supports all channels. Pretty much every channel that your users are interacting with you on are supported by Facebook analytics. The third point is provides cross-platform insights. So we're no longer providing individual insights about your app performance like Android performance, iOS performance, web performance and so on. If you're an e-commerce site you can for example have a lifetime value of customers across all channels and then it's integrated with Facebook apps tools and this is something that as we'll see in the product roadmap this is something that we are heavily invested in.
